title: Salt in Disguise: Why Everyday Foods Are Driving Silent Hypertension in Urban India
thbAction: Position THB's pharma CRM and omnichannel HCP engagement workflows to support launch planning, KAM orchestration, and compliant follow-up across India and the Middle East.
contentHtml:
• Industry
  • 5 min read

  • Brand Connect Initiative
  • ETHealthWorld
  • Published On Mar 31, 2026 at 11:26 AM IST
Most Indians believe they don’t eat too much salt because they don’t “add extra salt” at home. But the truth is — salt is no longer coming mainly from the kitchen. Today, sodium overload is coming from packaged snacks, ready-to-eat foods, restaurant meals, instant mixes, processed meats, breads, bakery items, chutneys, sauces, and even seemingly healthy foods like soups, protein snacks and salad dressings.This silent sodium load is one of the biggest reasons why even people who think they “eat normally” are developing borderline or high blood pressure in their late 30s and early 40s. The problem is not the visible salt on the plate — it is the salt hidden in food systems.Dr. ASHRAFUL HAQUE, Consultant- Interventional Cardiologist

Advt

In today’s diet patterns, hypertension is no longer coming from the salt we sprinkle on food — it is coming from the salt that is already inside processed and restaurant meals. People assume they are not consuming excess salt because they don’t add extra at home, but packaged snacks, breads, bakery items, instant mixes, chutneys, sauces and restaurant gravies are loaded with sodium. This chronic hidden sodium exposure is pushing people into early-stage hypertension without warning. We see 35- and 40-year-olds with elevated readings purely because of their food sourcing — not because of visible salt addiction

How Hidden Salt Increases Blood Pressure?

Sodium has a direct effect on the cardiovascular system. When there is excess sodium in the bloodstream, the body tries to dilute it by retaining water. This extra water increases the blood volume circulating inside the blood vessels. Higher volume means higher pressure. Over time, this constant pressure stiffens arterial walls. Eventually, it becomes hypertension.Hypertension rarely has early symptoms. It is called the “silent killer” for a reason — people continue their routine unaware that their heart is under increased strain daily. By the time symptoms appear, damage has already progressed.

Advt

Dr. RAMCHANDRA KUMAR, Consultant- Interventional Cardiologist For many patients, controlling sodium intake gives more benefit than adding another anti-hypertensive drug. Excess sodium disrupts the kidney’s ability to regulate fluid balance. As the body retains more water, blood volume increases and blood pressure rises even when the person looks fit or has normal BMI. Reducing sodium in daily meals — even by 15 to 20 percent — can bring blood pressure down within weeks. People need to check packaging labels more seriously and consciously choose low-sodium options. Small, consistent reductions have clinically significant results

Advt

Where is the Salt Coming From?

Most urban Indians consume way more sodium than they realise. Packaged foods are largely engineered to be “addictive” — salt enhances taste, shelf life and craving. Restaurant food, street food and fast casual dining are also heavily salted because salt enhances flavour fast.Even international food chains in India have far higher sodium levels in their India menus than the same brand in Europe or the US — because Indian taste buds now demand sharper flavour. All this creates a daily sodium overload.Dr. MADHUR JAIN, Consultant- Interventional Cardiologist When a relatively young patient in their late 30s or early 40s suddenly develops high blood pressure, one of the first questions I ask is about their weekly packaged food and dining-out frequency. More than 50 percent of hypertension cases in this age group today are driven by the modern food environment — not genetics. The human body is not designed to handle the high-sodium load present in current processed foods. Awareness is the first therapy. Before medication, correcting dietary sodium can prevent future complications like artery stiffening, kidney strain and increased risk of stroke..

Dining Behaviour Also Matters

Restaurant meals, office canteen food, delivery apps and weekend eating out have become routine lifestyle formats. Most such foods contain 2 to 3 times more sodium than a home cooked meal. Add pickles, papad, seasoning mixes, packaged masalas and instant soups — and the daily sodium level shoots up.Even some “healthy sounding” options like sandwiches, multigrain breads, grilled chicken rolls, ready protein snacks and flavoured nuts contain aggressively high sodium.

What Happens Post 40

After the age of 40, sodium sensitivity increases, especially in people who have sedentary routines or genetic tendency for blood pressure. The kidneys also become less efficient in clearing excess sodium. This is why blood pressure begins to rise even though lifestyle seems “the same.”Practical Ways to Control SodiumReducing salt does not mean food will become “tasteless.” It means being more aware and choosing smarter:
  • Choose freshly cooked food over packaged food
  • Reduce sauces, dips and chutneys (they have massive sodium)
  • Avoid topping salt on salads and fruits
  • Increase herbs, lemon, spices for flavour instead of extra salt
  • Read sodium content on packaged foods — most people never check this
Even a 20% reduction in daily sodium can bring down blood pressure readings in 6–8 weeks.

Conclusion

The modern salt problem is invisible. Most people don’t realise how much sodium they consume daily because processed food hides it cleverly. After 40, sodium awareness is a crucial cardiac strategy. Controlling salt intake is not about extreme restriction — it is about smarter choices.Protecting the heart today begins in the supermarket aisle, the menu selection and the condiment tray — not just in the kitchen.Disclaimer - The above content is non-editorial, and TIL hereby disclaims any and all warranties, expressed or implied, relating to it, and does not guarantee, vouch for or necessarily endorse any of the content.
